:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

No Data. Hawaii did not submit data to the National Corrections Reporting Program in 2020.

Prison Population Trends

The graph below provides a decade-long perspective on the prison system in Hawaii, highlighting three key metrics from 2010 to 2020. It showcases the overall prison population trend, comparing it with the number of individuals who were released from prison to parole, as well as those who met the eligibility criteria for parole but remained incarcerated.

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Hawaii has a largely indeterminate sentencing system in which the court sets only the maximum period of incarceration. Thus, when a person has been sentenced to an indeterminate or extended term of imprisonment, the Hawaii Paroling Authority must hold a hearing no later than six months after commitment to incarceration to determine the minimum term of imprisonment and issue a “tentative parole date” or “TPD.” This means that there is often a “minimum” hearing and a “parole release hearing” that are separate. The TPD can later be reduced at the discretion of the Authority with sixty days’ notice to the prosecuting attorney. Note that the Authority can only hear felony cases; misdemeanants are given determinate sentences fixed by the court. The parole guidelines determine the earliest potential release date. Unless good cause is shown, offenders who have been assessed as low-risk must be granted parole at the earliest guideline release date.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, there were NA people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes and with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up NA% of the prison population.

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Gender

(1) bar charts including community, prison pop, parole-eligible pop, paroled at first opportunity pop (2) RRIs for each of those populations, (3) release date compared to first eligibility date (e.g., 50% released first year, 22% year after first eligibility, 15% 2 years after eligible, etc.)

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Race and Ethnicity

For every White person in prison, there are 3.1 Black, non-Hispanic people in prison.
``` ::: :::
![](C:/Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/ggplots/rri_infograph_Georgia.png){ width=200px }
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Note: The Relative Rate Index (RRI) measures racial and ethnic disparities by comparing rates between groups, often using White individuals as the reference. The RRI provides insight into the degree of overrepresentation or underrepresentation, with an RRI greater than 1 indicating that a particular racial or ethnic group is disproportionately represented. Source: BJS Prisoners (2020), Census (2020)

Predicted Probabilities

Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date.
